title: How to Set Up Uptime Kuma on a k3s Kubernetes Cluster
excerpt: Discover how to set up Uptime Kuma on a k3s Kubernetes cluster with this step-by-step guide. Utilize MetalLB for local IP assignment, create necessary YAML configuration files, and deploy Uptime Kuma. Monitor your services efficiently on your local network.
content: In this article, I'll explain how to set up Uptime Kuma on a k3s Kubernetes cluster. I'll be using MetalLB on my k3s cluster to assign LAN IP addresses. You can check out my article explaining how to do this: ## Prerequisites {#prerequisites} Before you begin, ensure you have the following: - A running k3s Kubernetes cluster. - MetalLB installed and configured on your k3s cluster to assign LAN IP addresses. Refer to my [MetalLB article](/network-metallb/) for detailed steps. ## Step 1: Create Configuration Files {#step-1-create-configuration-files} First, create a directory for your Uptime Kuma configurations. Inside this directory, you will create three YAML files: 1. `uptime-kuma-loadbalancer.yaml` 2. `uptime-kuma-pvc.yaml` 3. `uptime-kuma.yaml` ### 1\. `uptime-kuma-loadbalancer.yaml` {#1-uptime-kuma-loadbalanceryaml} This file defines a LoadBalancer service to expose Uptime Kuma on your local network. ```yaml apiVersion: v1 kind: Service metadata: name: uptime-lb spec: selector: app: uptime-kuma ports: - port: 3001 targetPort: 3001 type: LoadBalancer loadBalancerIP: 192.168.2.210 ``` ### 2\. `uptime-kuma-pvc.yaml` {#2-uptime-kuma-pvcyaml} This file defines a PersistentVolumeClaim to provide storage for Uptime Kuma. ```yaml apiVersion: v1 kind: PersistentVolumeClaim metadata: name: uptime-kuma-pvc spec: accessModes: - ReadWriteOnce resources: requests: storage: 1Gi ``` ### 3\. `uptime-kuma.yaml` {#3-uptime-kumayaml} This file defines the Uptime Kuma deployment. ```yaml apiVersion: apps/v1 kind: Deployment metadata: name: uptime-kuma labels: app: uptime-kuma spec: replicas: 1 selector: matchLabels: app: uptime-kuma template: metadata: labels: app: uptime-kuma spec: containers: - name: uptime-kuma image: louislam/uptime-kuma:latest ports: - containerPort: 3001 volumeMounts: - name: kuma-volume mountPath: /app/data volumes: - name: kuma-volume persistentVolumeClaim: claimName: uptime-kuma-pvc ``` ## Step 2: Apply the Configurations {#step-2-apply-the-configurations} Open a terminal in the directory containing your YAML files and run the following command to apply all configurations: ```bash kubectl apply -f ./ ``` ## Step 3: Verify the Deployment {#step-3-verify-the-deployment} Check the status of your deployment to ensure everything is running correctly: ``` Kubectl get all -o wide ``` ## Accessing Uptime Kuma {#accessing-uptime-kuma} Once your deployment is running, you should be able to access Uptime Kuma on your local network at http://192.168.2.210:3001.
